nativeRange.habitat: Relatively slow-flowing sections of forested, well-oxygenated headwaters with clear water; mixed sand and rock substrate with submerged driftwood and leaf litter. Has also been documented in medium to large rivers and flooded fields.

diet.notes: Readily accepts sinking pellets, nano pellets, gel foods, frozen bloodworm, Daphnia, and Artemia. Has been documented consuming small aquatic snails and invertebrates, making it a useful pest-snail controller. Blanched vegetables are generally accepted. Live or frozen chironomid larvae are particularly relished.

avoidWith: long-finned or slow-moving species (bettas, fancy guppies)

imageCandidates.notes: Stefan Maurer, 1303x869. Sharp, well-lit single specimen clearly showing the golden dorsal chain/reticulated pattern, dark markings and silvery belly on a vibrant live fish.
Lerdsuwa, 1500x1000. Two individuals together (good for this schooling species) with correct chain pattern; slightly soft/shot-through-glass so kept as a non-recommended group shot.
NasserHalaweh, 1068x801. Live individual in a leaf-litter biotope; loach is somewhat distant and a guppy shares the frame, so kept only as a lower-ranked extra.

summary: The Dwarf Chain Loach is generally considered the smallest member of the Botiidae family and among its most peaceful, making it well suited to community tanks. Native to Thailand, it is best kept in groups of at least six and is valued for its ability to control pest snails.
careNotes:
Dwarf Chain Loaches are generally regarded as the smallest members of the family Botiidae and among the most peaceful of the Botia relatives. They tend to form complex social hierarchies and are generally advised to be kept in groups of at least 6, with 10 or more often recommended for stable social behavior. Solitary or under-grouped specimens have been known to become withdrawn or show stress-related symptoms.

Though generally peaceful, some long-term keepers have reported occasional fin nipping toward very slow or long-finned species — sedentary tankmates and fish with flowing fins are best avoided as a precaution.

This species has been reclassified from Botia sidthimunki to Ambastaia sidthimunki, and is sometimes listed under the former name in older resources. Precise locality data within Thailand is generally withheld from public records as a conservation measure.

Ambastaia sidthimunki is listed as Endangered (EN) on the IUCN Red List, historically impacted by over-collection for the aquarium trade. Captive breeding via hormonal induction is now established at commercial scale, and the vast majority of specimens in the trade are captive-bred. Private hobbyist breeding is rarely documented; one known account (Mark Duffill) achieved spawning in a 284-liter tank with a large group and slightly acidic water.

Like other Botiids, this species is scaleless and is generally considered sensitive to copper-based medications — copper treatments should be avoided and copper-free alternatives used instead. It possesses sharp sub-ocular spines that can catch in nets; a cup or container is recommended for transfers. A tight-fitting lid is advisable as individuals have been known to jump.

Weekly water changes of 30–50% are generally advised. The species tends to swim in the midwater and upper column rather than hugging the substrate — an unusual trait for a loach.
breedingNotes: Commercial breeding via hormonal induction is now well-established, with most trade specimens captive-bred. Spontaneous breeding in home aquaria is very rarely documented. One confirmed private-hobbyist case involved a large group (36 individuals) in a 284-liter tank; the keeper reportedly manipulated pH from 7.6 down to approximately 6.8 and provided rock pile spawning sites. Varying the diet and conditioning with live/frozen foods are generally suggested as breeding triggers, consistent with practices used for related Botiids.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish
additional.notes: Cross-checked size, temperature, pH, tank size, native range, diet, and grouping. SF gives temperature 20–30°C vs Co-Op 24–28°C; used 22–28°C (brief guidance), consistent with both. SF gives minimum tank 72 L (80×30 cm base); Co-Op recommends 20 gal (~76 L); rounded up to 100 L given active schooling species needing 6+ specimens. SF notes native range as Mae Klong + Ataran drainages; FishBase adds Chao Phraya and Mekong basins — disagreement documented in careNotes. Minimum group confirmed at 5–6 (SF) / 7–10 (Co-Op); used 6 as floor.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed family Botiidae, order Cypriniformes, adult size ~5.5 cm SL, IUCN Endangered (EN) status, temperature 26–28°C, pH 6.0–8.0. Used as tertiary cross-check for taxonomy and IUCN status.

fish.breedingNotes: Spontaneous breeding in home aquaria is essentially undocumented. Commercial production relies on hormonal induction. One confirmed case of private hobbyist spawning has been reported.
